topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of disappointment. I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening until a dull ache radiated across my temples. The team selection roster was posted, and my name wasn't on it. I reread the notes, the carefully crafted justifications for the chosen players, and a sour taste flooded my mouth. They had clearly overlooked me. I let out a slow, almost silent breath, the air tasting stale in my lungs. I picked up my phone, not to call anyone, but just to feel the cool glass in my palm. The sheer audacity of it—the belief that I was somehow less valuable than those selected—made my shoulders slump. I didn't bother deleting the email; I just closed the window, the click echoing the finality of the rejection.

[story 2]

"Well, isn't this a joke?" I muttered, the words barely audible over the low hum of the gym ventilation. I watched the others—the ones who got the call, the ones who got the glory—laughing easily. Their camaraderie felt like a physical barrier separating me from their world. I walked over to the water fountain, deliberately splashing cold water onto my face, a sharp, stinging sensation that momentarily cleared the fog of my frustration. It wasn't anger that fueled the gesture; it was a deep-seated feeling that the entire system was rigged, designed to exclude people like me. I turned away from the group, my posture stiff, every muscle coiled tight with resentment toward the perceived unfairness of the situation.

[story 3]

The coach’s announcement was delivered in a monotone that scraped against my nerves. When my name wasn't called, I didn't move. I stood there, hands shoved deep into my pockets, feeling the heat rise in my neck. I observed the congratulations being showered on the selected squad, a wave of icy detachment washing over me. I watched them high-five, their smiles wide and effortless, and I felt a distinct tightening in my chest, a hard knot of self-reproach mixed with a profound sense of being utterly dismissed. I finally turned, the movement sharp and jerky, and walked toward the exit without a backward glance, the sound of my own footsteps echoing hollowly on the polished floor.
